coredata.dc:title: Development of an eco-friendly fluorescence nanosensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer on silica-carbon quantum dot for the rapid indoxacarb detection
coredata.dc:creator: Shirani, Marziyeh Poshteh
Rezaei, Behzad
Ensafi, Ali A.
Ramezani, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and efficient detection of indoxacarb (IXC), a common chemical contaminant, in environmental and biological samples is necessary. In this work, a modern optical sensor was developed for IXC, based on environmentally friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated on silica-carbon quantum dots (SiCQDs). A hydrothermal method was used to prepare highly fluorescence SiCQDs and, subsequently, MIP formed on surface (MIP@SiCQDs) using a sol-gel method. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching effect and increased IXC concentration was found for the range of 4–102 nM, under the optimal conditions, with a 1 nM detection limit. Precisions was of 4.5 and 2.3% for five replicate detections at 21 and 60 nM IXC, respectively. Applicability of the sensor for IXC quantification in environmental and biological samples was verified with recoveries in the range 95–106% with a relative standard deviation of <6.0%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Indoxacarb
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorimetric sensor
Sol-gel method
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs nanocomposites
Template measurement and a calibration curve

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of SiCQDs, (B) DLS test of the SiCQDs, (C) FT-IR of SiCQDs, (D) XRD diagram of SiCQDs, (E) UV–Vis spectrum of the SiCQDs and (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of the SiCQDs,
TEM images of (A) MIP@SiCQDs, (B) NIP@SiCQDs and (C) FT-IR spectra of MIP@SiCQDs (a) and NIP@SiCQDs (b).

(A) Effect of pH (in the range of 4.0–11.0) on the response of the 0.5 mg mL−1 MIP@SiCQDs to 40 nM of IXC, (B) Effect of temperature on the response of the 0.5 mg mL−1 MIP@SiCQDs to 40 nM of IXC at pH 7.0, (C) Effect of the different amount of MIP@SiCQDs (of 0.5–3.0 mg mL−1) on the response in the presence of 40 nM IXC at pH 7.0 and 25 °C. (D) Effect of time on the response of the 1.5 mg mL−1 MIP@SiCQDs to 40 nM of IXC at pH 7.0 and 25 °C.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) MIP@SiCQDs, (B) NIP@SiCQDs with the addition of different concentrations of IXC (4.0–102), The Stern–Volmer plots for (C) MIP@SiCQDs and (D) NIP@SiCQDs with different concentrations of IXC at the optimum conditions.
The measurement of IXC in well water, apple and tomato samples using the MIP@SiCQDs sensor.
Comparison of the developed sensor with the reported methods for the detection of IXC in real samples.

head.ce:title: Development of an eco-friendly fluorescence nanosensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer on silica-carbon quantum dot for the rapid indoxacarb detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and efficient detection of indoxacarb (IXC), a common chemical contaminant, in environmental and biological samples is necessary. In this work, a modern optical sensor was developed for IXC, based on environmentally friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated on silica-carbon quantum dots (SiCQDs). A hydrothermal method was used to prepare highly fluorescence SiCQDs and, subsequently, MIP formed on surface (MIP@SiCQDs) using a sol-gel method. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching effect and increased IXC concentration was found for the range of 4–102 nM, under the optimal conditions, with a 1 nM detection limit. Precisions was of 4.5 and 2.3% for five replicate detections at 21 and 60 nM IXC, respectively. Applicability of the sensor for IXC quantification in environmental and biological samples was verified with recoveries in the range 95–106% with a relative standard deviation of <6.0%.

ce:section.ce:para: Indoxacarb (IXC) is an oxadiazine pesticides used extensively on a range of crops including cotton, sweet corn (maize), lettuce, vegetables and fruit such as apples, pears, and cherries for controlling Lepidoptera, cockroaches, and ants. IXC blocks the sodium ion flow into nerve cells by binding to sites on sodium channels (Brugger, 1997). This pesticide is categorized as safe in mammals due to its hydrophobicity and low penetration into natural waters (Wing et al., 2000). However, the application of IXC has been reported to affect non-target organisms such as bees and aquatic organisms (fish) (Pashte & Patil, 2018; Taheri Mirghaed, Ghelichpour, Mirzargar, Joshaghani, & Ebrahimzadeh Mousavi, 2018). Commonly, various analytical methods can be used for the accurate and selective detection of IXC, such as gas chromatography (GC) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with mass spectrometry (Dzuman, Zachariasova, Veprikova, Godula, & Hajslova, 2015; Saidi, Mouhouche, & Abri, 2017; Słowik-Borowiec, Szpyrka, & Walorczyk, 2015; Tette et al., 2016). These methods are expensive, complex, and time-consuming. Therefore, the development of a helpful rapid, and efficient method is important for the detection of IXC in biological and environmental samples.

An interesting group of carbon-based quantum dots, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently gained increasing attention. CQDs are preferred over traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) due to their excellent biocompatibility, high stability, strong resistance to photodegradation, low toxicity, simple synthesis, and tunable excitation and emission spectra. Therefore, they are considered as excellent response signal candidates for designing sensors (Liu et al., 2016; Shariati, Rezaei, Jamei, & Ensafi, 2019; Zhou, Gao, Liu, Wang, & Chu, 2017). Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) are attractive compounds for the preparation of materials containing tailor-made binding sites, which are highly selective toward target molecules with properties similar to those of biological antibodies. MIPs are usually synthesized by copolymerization of functional and crosslinking monomers in the attendance of a target (Beltran, Borrull, Marcé, & Cormack, 2010; Turiel & Martín-Esteban, 2010). Upon the removal of the template, complementary imprinting cavities whose shape, size and functional group fit the target molecule were formed in the MIPs, allowing the template to reenter the MIPs (Chao, Hu, & Chen, 2016).

Among different MIP preparation methods, sol-gel polymerization has been of great interest due to some of its distinguished advantages such as low cost, the simplicity of preparation, green synthetic method and mild polymerization conditions (Mao, Bao, Han, Li, & Niu, 2012). Novel techniques for selective detection and analysis of a target species can be investigated by a combination of the high selectivity of MIP and the efficient fluorescence characteristics of CQDs. Fluorescence intensity changes upon binding of a target species to the imprinted cavities in such CQDs based sensing systems. QDs coated with MIP film by the sol-gel polymerization have already been reported (Ensafi et al., 2017a, 2017b). However, QDs have to be covered with organosilane prior to the sol-gel hydrolysis and condensation imprinted polymerization to improve the interaction between MIP and QDs. These two steps are required in the polymer preparation process.

In this work, SiCQDs have been coated with a silica imprinted layer through a one-step, sol-gel reaction. Since silica is inert and transparent, the fluorescence properties of SiCQDs are maintained (Poshteh Shirani et al., 2017). Highly fluorescence SiCQDs were organized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) (Chen et al., 2014). MIP nanocomposites could be prepared via a simple and efficient method using a mixture of SiCQDs, template molecules (IXC), functional monomer (APTES), and cross-linker (tetraethoxysilane, TEOS) through hydrolysis and condensation process in the attendance of ammonia solution as the catalyst (Mao et al., 2012). Thus, a green, fast, simple and selective IXC optical sensor has been developed by the one step, sol-gel process based on anchoring the MIP matrix on the SiCQD surface.

Citric acid, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), ammonium hydroxide (25–28%) and ethanol were supplied by Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. (London, UK). Indoxacarb (IXC) was obtained from Aria Shimi Co. (Zahedan, Iran). Doubly distilled water was used in all experiments.

A Jasco FP–750 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan) was utilized to carry out fluorescence measurements. The Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra with the KBr pellet technique (at 4000–400 cm−1) were registered on a Jasco 680-plus FT-IR spectrophotometer (Japan). The absorbance spectra were taken on a Jasco V-570 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken from a Philips CM120 (Netherland). The SiCQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using an Asenware AW-XDM300 (China) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument Malvern ZEN3600 (UK).

Water-soluble SiCQDs were synthesized by modifications of a previously reported method (Chen et al., 2014). 500 µL of APTES and 1.0 g of citric acid were dissolved in deionized water and the solution was then transferred into an autoclave and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. The yellowish solution was obtained after the reaction process. Finally, the product was purified by dialysis for 24 h and the SiCQDs solution obtained was collected and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Sol-gel method was used to prepare the imprinting polymer (Mao et al., 2012) at the surface of the SiCQDs. 30 mg of IXC was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol under sonication, followed by mixing with 60 µL of APTES. Then 400 μL of SiCQDs added and mixed for 10 min. Afterward, 100 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of ammonium hydroxide were successively added and the mixture was stirred for 24 h at ambient temperature. The MIP@SiCQDs nanocomposites formed were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm) for 5 min. A solvent mixture of ethanol/water was used to discard the template molecules (IXC) and unreacted reagents. The template removing from the MIP@SiCQDs was followed with UV–Vis spectroscopy. The pure MIP@SiCQDs were dried under vacuum at 50 °C. The non-imprinted particles (NIP@SiCQDs) were used to assess the molecular recognition properties of the MIP under the like conditions without adding template molecules (IXC).

A mixture containing 1.5 mg/mL of the MIP@SiCQDs nanocomposite in the (Britton-Robinson buffer pH 7, 0.04 M) was dispersed in order to quantitatively measure IXC. A known volume of IXC standard solution was then added to the prepare mixture. Fluorescence measurements were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the emission range of 360–600 nm. The fluorescence signal (at λmax = 410) was registered in the presence and absence of IXC and introduced to as F and F0, respectively. The F0/F ratio was utilized as a response function in the evaluation of IXC concentration.

The water sample collected from well water located at Saman city (Shahrkoord, Iran). Well water sample was filtered to remove suspended materials through a filter 0.22 μm (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). Apple and tomato were purchased from a local supermarket (Isfahan, Iran). To make the fruit samples, the samples were first homogenized in a blender (Moulinex LM238 Blender, France). Then a 10 g of each sample was weighed into a centrifuge tube, followed by centrifugation for 10 min at 6000 rpm. The supernatant was used without extra pretreatment. The apple and tomato samples were diluted 10 times with the Britton-Robinson buffer solution while well water samples were not diluted. The standard addition method was used for the detection of IXC in the real samples. Because the concentration of IXC in all of the real samples was less than the detection limit of the method, the recovery test, and the measurement accuracy was performed in the spiked samples.

TEM and DLS analyses were used to confirm the morphology and size of the prepared SiCQDs. As observed in Fig. 1 (A), the SiCQDs with an average size of 3.3 ± 0.4 nm are well distributed with a spherical shape. The size distribution, estimated using DLS test, was 3.1 ± 0.5 nm (Fig 0.1(B)). FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the surface functional groups on SiCQDs (Fig. 1(C)). The characteristic peaks at 3380 and 3150 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, respectively. The bands at 1700 and 1560 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively, and the peaks at 2920 and 1360 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of CH. The band at 1100 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of Si–O. Fig. 1(D) shows the XRD pattern of SiCQDs in the 10–100° range. The two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.2 and 44.05° (Fig. 1(D)), which verify the crystalline graphite-like structure of the prepared SiCQDs (Wang et al., 2016). UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were carried out to evaluate the optical properties of the synthetic SiCQDs (Fig. 1(E)). The absorption spectrum displays a maximum band at about 340 nm, which is associated with π–π* transitions of CC and CO. A maximum emission signal was observed at 410 nm under the excitation of 340 nm in the fluorescence spectrum of the prepared SiCQDs (Fig. 1(F)).

TEM was used to study the morphology of MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs nanocomposites. MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs particles were similar and both exhibited spherical nanostructure (Fig. 2 (A and B)). Fig. 2(C) displays the FT-IR spectra of MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs. The peaks at 3393 and 1572 cm−1 are related to the NH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The bonds around 2934 and 1395 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1130 and 1050 cm−1 are due to SiOC and Si–O–Si bonds, respectively. The peaks at about 460 and 790 cm−1 are ascribed to the Si–O asymmetric stretching vibration. No distinct differences were observed between the major bands in the FT-IR spectra of MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs, signifying the similar compositions of these compounds.

A single step synthetic method has been proposed for the preparation of a MIP doped with SiCQDs. IXC template molecules communicated with APTES by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces and further copolymerized with surface functionalized SiCQDs. Thus, the templates were successfully inserted in the silica coated matrix. APTES and TEOS were thus copolymerized on the surface of silica coated SiCQDs using aqueous ammonia as the catalyst (Li, Li, & Cheng, 2010). MIP@SiCQDs nanocomposites containing specific IXC imprinted sites were obtained following the removal of unreacted starting materials and template by washing with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water. MIP@SiCQDs fluorescence intensity was about 18% that of NIP@SiCQDs (Fig. S1). However, the fluorescence intensity of MIP@SiCQDs was restored to 96% that of NIP@SiCQDs following the removal of IXC (template molecule) by solvent extraction, indicating the effective removal of IXC from MIP@SiCQDs.

Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of MIP@SiCQDs was quenched by IXC introduction. So, the quenching mechanism of MIP@SiCQDs toward IXC was studied. Since the UV–Vis absorption peak of IXC (Fig S2) has a good spectral overlap with the excitation and emission band of the MIP@SiCQDs (fluorophore). Thus, it appears that there can be appropriate absorber and fluorophore pair for the high-efficiency inner filter effect (IFE) (Yang et al., 2018; Yang, Wu, Lin, & Huang, 2018). Thus, the IXC can not only protect the excitation light of MIP@SiCQDs but also absorb the emission signal from the MIP@SiCQDs, so it was affirmed that the IFE happen in a well effective method.

Some assay conditions, which may influence the efficiency of fluorescence quenching, such as the incubation time, pH, MIP@SiCQDs amount and temperature were studied and optimized using quenching ratio defined as F0/F to obtain a high sensitivity for IXC determination.

MIP@SiCQDs are sensitive to pH value and their surrounding significantly affects the fluorescence intensity of MIP@SiCQDs. The binding efficiency of MIP@SiCQDs and target analyte are affected by very acidic or alkaline media (Ren & Chen, 2015). Thus, the effect of the buffer solution (Britton-Robinson buffer) pH applied to prepare MIP@SiCQDs solution in the 4.0–11.0 range was studied. As observed in Fig. 3 A, pH had an important effect on the fluorescence quenching of MIP@SiCQDs, the most sensitivity being had at pH 7.0. The sensitivity reduced at lower pH’s because of the presence of hydrogen ion in the solution, which affects the hydrogen bonding between MIP@SiCQDs and IXC (Chullasat, Nurerk, Kanatharana, Davis, & Bunkoed, 2018). At pH values of over 7.0, the sensitivity also decreased, probably due to the hydrolysis of the template molecule (Fengxia & Pei, 2008). Further, the silica shell ionized under the highly basic condition and caused damaging the binding site structure. (Hou et al., 2016). Therefore, a Britton-Robinson buffer solution of pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal value and used in subsequent experiments.

The effect of temperature (5–50 °C) on the MIP@SiCQDs fluorescence quenching has been studied. As observed in Fig. 3B, at temperatures of over 25 °C the fluorescence quenching of the MIP@SiCQDs, decreased with increasing temperature. In addition, the fluorescence quenching of the MIP@SiCQDs decreased with decreasing temperature to under 25 °C. At low temperatures, the molecules do not have enough energy to access the MIP@SiCQDs and form hydrogen bonds with its surface. Therefore, the further studies were performed at 25 °C.

Excess MIP@SiCQDs causes low sensitivity to IXC. However, an insufficient amount may lead to the narrow linear range (Xu, Lu, Li, Song, Wang, Chen, et al., 2013). Thus, 0.5–3 mg/mL quantities of MIP@SiCQDs were used to study its effect on fluorescence quenching. 1.5 mg/mL of MIP@SiCQDs gave the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3C). Decreasing the amount of MIP@SiCQDs gave rise to a lower sensitivity due to the reduction of the signal to noise ratio. Moreover, the sensitivity reduced upon using MIP@SiCQDs quantities of over 1.5 mg/mL and occurrence of self-absorption of the fluorescence. Consequently, 1.5 mg/mL was applied as the optimum MIP@SiCQDs quantity.

The fluorescence quenching ratio of IXC and MIP@SiCQDs was used to investigate their interaction time. The signal intensity changes of MIP@SiCQDs improved up to an incubation time of 5 min and remained constant afterward (Fig. 3D). Thus, an incubation time of 5 min was chosen for next study.

The reusability of MIP@SiCQDs sensor was investigated by observing the change of the fluorescence quenching at 40 nM fixed IXC solution concentration for 10 times. MIP@SiCQDs could be recovered by centrifugation, followed by washing with ethanol/water solvent mixture to remove the rebinding IXC. MIP@SiCQDs sensor can be reused in IXC detection without any appreciable loss of fluorescence quenching (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Template measurement and a calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectra of the MIP@SiCQDs and NIP@SiCQDs in the attendance of different IXC concentrations were studied to compare the recognition capability of the imprinted SiCQDs with that of non-imprinted SiCQDs. The fluorescence intensity of MIP@SiCQDs obviously decreased with increasing IXC concentration (Fig. 4 A) while the NIP@SiCQDs fluorescence intensity did not significantly change (Fig. 4B). The specific binding affinity of MIP@SiCQDs towards IXC due to the cavities in MIP@SiCQDs is responsible for their higher quenching efficiency or better sensitivity. The Stern-Volmer equation can be quantified the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the MIP@SiCQDs and IXC concentration (Ensafi, Nasr-Esfahani, & Rezaei, 2018).
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C
IXC
which F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities prior to and following IXC addition, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and CIXC is the IXC added concentration. The Stern-Volmer plot is displayed in Fig. 4 (C and D). A linear response to IXC in the range of 4–102 nM is shown by MIP@SiCQDs, the limit of detection (LOD) being 1 nM. Five replicated analyses of 21 and 60 nM solutions were used to calculate the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 4.5% and 2.3%, respectively. Imprinting factor (IF) is a significant parameter in the evaluation of the selectivity of the MIP (Wang, He, Ji, & Yan, 2009; Xu et al., 2013). IF is generally quantified using IF = KMIP/KNIP, where KMIP and KNIP are the linear slopes corresponding to MIP and NIP calibration curves, respectively. Under optimal conditions, the IF of the sensor was 2.6, which showed that the MIP@SiCQDs was much more selective than NIP@SiCQDs.

Fluorescence quenching response to IXC was used to further assess the selectivity of the fabricated MIP@SiCQDs and the results were compared with those obtained by NIP@SiCQDs and bare SiCQDs. Compounds analogous to IXC such as difenoconazole, imidacloprid, dichlorvos, fenitrothion, acetamiprid, and chlorpyrifos (at concentrations of 4.0 µM) were tested for this purpose. The selectivity of MIP@SiCQDs for IXC detection is much better than that of NIP@SiCQDs and bare SiCQDs (Fig. S4) because IXC interacts with a great affinity with the imprinted cavities in the MIP@SiCQDs whereas there are no selective recognition cavities in the NIP@SiCQDs and bare SiCQDs.

An analytical method is acceptable when it is applicable in the analysis of real samples. The ability of the MIP@SiCQDs sensor to measure IXC in well water, tomato, and apple was investigated. The linear range of the concentration of IXC in the real samples was 4.7–102 nM. No IXC was found by HPLC in the real samples. Thus, IXC was spiked to the real samples. The levels selected could cover all linear ranges of the suggested sensor. Therefore, this sensor can be used for quantification of IXC in the well water, apple, and tomato in this concentration range. The standard addition method was used to eliminate the matrix effects since the matrix affects all of the solutions equally. This method can be applied in a complex matrix. Table 1 shows the results of IXC determination for each sample. The IXC recoveries were in the range of 95–106% with RSD of less than 6, showing that the proposed sensor can be used for the measurement of IXC in real samples.

The accuracy of the sensor in the analysis of IXC in the real samples of well water, apple, and tomato samples was assessed by the comparison of the results from the suggested and standard (HPLC) methods (Din, Azab, Almaz, Gaaboub, & Soliman, 2015). Table S5 shows the statistical results. The Comparison of the results indicates that there is no significant difference between these two analytical methods.
The figures of merit including linear dynamic range, limit of detection, recovery, precision, and response time of this sensor with some other MIP-QDs and MIP-CQD sensors are given in Table S6.

The results show that the linear dynamic range and detection limit of this sensor are comparable or better than those of QD/MS/MIP, paper@QDs@MIPs, CdSeTe/ZnS@MIP, MIP@SiC-dot, CQD@MIPs, MIP-C-dots and CDs@SiO2@MIPs. In addition, the detection time of the developed sensor is lower or comparable to that of those reported in the literature. Therefore, the MIP@SiCQDs could be used as a novel fluorescence sensor for the rapid detection of IXC in real samples.

Table 2 compares the analytical figures of merit of this sensor with some other reported analytical sensors for quantification of IXC in real samples such as water, apple, and tomato. The developed sensor is more sensitive compared with HPLC and GC methods, as observed (Din et al., 2015; Lesueur, Knittl, Gartner, Mentler, & Fuerhacker, 2008; Mandal et al., 2020; Sakthiselvi, Paramasivam, Vasanthi, & Bhuvaneswari, 2020; Sannino, Bolzoni, & Bandini, 2004; Tiryaki, 2016). In addition, the present method does not require expensive to sophisticated instrumentation or highly skilled operators, unlike chromatographic methods. The main advantages of this method are the speed, simplicity and reliable detection of IXC.

In this work, a quick, simple and efficient strategy has been developed for the synthesis of the environmentally friendly, molecularly imprinted fluorescence materials based on SiCQDs to selectively determine IXC. The detection is based on the fluorescence quenching response of the MIP@SiCQDs following the IXC binding into the specific cavities of MIP@SiCQDs. The prepared MIP@SiCQDs combine the exclusive properties of SiCQDs and excellent selectivity of MIP. The other advantages of this approach include low cost, facile preparation, and low detection limit. The performance of the sensor has been shown by IXC detection in real samples.
Marziyeh Poshteh Shirani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Behzad Rezaei: Supervision. Ali A. Ensafi: Investigation. Mohammad Ramezani: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127920.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
